notes.anchor: Decimus Brutus's arrival at his legions
notes.note: Decimus Junius Brutus Albinus, one of the assassins, had been assigned Cisalpine Gaul and its legions by Caesar; Cicero sees the army he is taking up as the republicans' best military hope against Antony.
Sextus Pompeius, the surviving son of Pompey the Great, was still in arms in Spain; Cicero reckons that his refusal to lay them down makes a renewed civil war certain.

Cicero held a libera legatio ("free legation"), a nominal commission as a legate originally granted by Caesar, which let a senator travel abroad with official standing; he is weighing whether to use it to slip away to Greece.
Cicero's nephew, Quintus Cicero the younger (son of his brother Quintus), at this time aligned with Antony; he is the gossiping source for the rumour about the villa and the dowry.
Aquilia, the prospective bride whose dowry is at issue in the marriage scheme Cicero relays; she reappears in 14.17 as a "stepmother," the marriage having gone forward.

Q. Egnatius is the lender (or guarantor) to whom Cicero's brother Quintus owes the dowry money he is straining to repay; no entity for this particular Quintus Egnatius is registered, so the surface is left unbound and flagged.
Sextus Clodius, an agent of the demagogue P. Clodius, had been exiled; Antony recalled him by claiming the measure was among Caesar's enactments, exemplifying the abuse Cicero condemns next.

Antony was producing measures he claimed to find in Caesar's papers (the acta Caesaris); Cicero charges that these commentarii were forged, smuggling through decisions the living Caesar would never have countenanced.
